source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
Carl composed the figure shown on the left side of the drawing from the smaller three-square and four-square figures shown on the right side. The smaller figures can be turned around, but not turned over. What is the smallest number of three-square figures needed for that?
<image1>

image_dependency: question_text explicitly refers to 'the figure shown on the left side of the drawing' and smaller figures on the right, so the target shape and pieces are not recoverable from text alone
the task depends on visual shape structure and allowable rotations of the component figures
multi_step: solving requires identifying the large figure and the available 3-square and 4-square pieces from the image
the condition 'can be turned around, but not turned over' introduces orientation constraints beyond simple counting
the question asks for the smallest number of three-square figures needed, implying combinatorial composition/optimization rather than a single direct lookup
verifiability: answer_type is numeric and answer_text='2', so the final response format is precise
registry_passed=true, with image and answer both present
the claim is in principle checkable by tiling/area-and-shape verification against the pictured pieces, though verification still depends on the image

decision_reasons: question, image, and numeric answer are all present, so the core semantic assets are intact
registry_passed is true and no asset issues are recorded
although the problem is highly image-dependent, the required image is available
the sample has good downstream value: multi-step spatial reasoning with a precise, verifiable numeric answer
